pageTitle.value: El Anatsui & Bronwyn Katz  - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: El Anatsui and South African artist Bronwyn Katz share an interest in reinventing discarded materials to provide a reflection on humanity, history and the planet.

WaPageTitle.value: El Anatsui & Bronwyn Katz  - Rolex mentoring programme

metadescription.value: Mentor El Anatsui and artist Bronwyn Katz share their passion for reinventing discarded materials to create art that reflects humanity. More on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: El Anatsui & Bronwyn Katz  - Rolex mentoring programme

text.value: El Anatsui and South African artist Bronwyn Katz share an interest in reinventing discarded materials to provide a reflection on humanity, history and the planet. Contributing to renewing the world is the most important responsibility of an artist, says the distinguished Ghanaian sculptor. Here, they each relate how they experienced their relationship.

“I’ve been inspired by El’s work since my days as a university student. He was one of the first artists from the African continent that I learned about who was working with reclaimed materials. His work deals with caring for the Earth and reappropriating material waste is a part of that care. I am invested in environmental justice and work with salvaged materials and the residues and traces of materials. Conceptually, El and I have many threads that connect our work.

We have a shared language. When I was visiting El at his studio in Ghana we had conversations about water bodies, childhood memories and myths of water bodies and how these seep into each of our respective practices. There is something curious and playful about the flexibility of water. I think that both El and I work with a similar type of play, as water does, in the sense that we change the personality of our found objects through structural changes.

El is consistently challenging himself, trying to go beyond what he’s done before. He is not afraid of being inconsistent.

A good mentor is somebody who finds joy in supporting and encouraging the endeavours of their mentees. They foster generosity, which is essential for reciprocity and non-hierarchical knowledge exchange. A good mentor is not necessarily someone who delegates tasks or activities to their mentee, they are individuals who are curious about the people who seek their mentorship and use the wisdom they have garnered to help their mentees find their own paths.”

title.value: A good mentor is somebody who finds joy in supporting and encouraging the endeavours of their mentees. They foster generosity, which is essential for reciprocity and non-hierarchical knowledge exchange.

text.value:
“I grew up in rural Ghana – then called the Gold Coast. At that time, there were no museums or galleries, no art as I understand it now. I didn’t know that people lived and worked as artists throughout their lives. When I was at art school, my uncle, who was my guardian and mentor said: ‘I have a feeling that when you finish, you will teach at the university.’ That prediction did come true because I taught at the university for close to 50 years.

Bronwyn takes almost any object that has been worn out and probably discarded, and she tries to give it a new kind of life, which is similar to what I do. The only difference is that she might work on material once and move onto something else, whereas in my case, I stick to the same material and turn it round and round and round and do so many things to get something – multiplying and reaching infinite possibilities.

As an artist, you ask yourself, are you adding something to what you have made? Or you are just repeating what you have made? In the process of being inquisitive, you discover that you have opened people’s eyes to think of other ways in which a material can be used.

When I met Bronwyn, I thought that someone like that would be very focused on work. She is not a talkative type, which is very much like me, too. She is somebody who is very driven, which is a very good quality for any artist to have. An artist is, first of all, self-selected and being self-selected, you have got to drive yourself.

title.value: As an artist, you ask yourself, are you adding something to what you have made? Or you are just repeating what you have made?

text.value: I like working with a material that has been used in ways that are not creative. It lets people see that there is possibility to engage with things that we do not consider very important. It’s a very minor way of doing it, but in that way, I can contribute to renewing the world, which I think is the most important responsibility of an artist.”

pageTitle.value: Anne Lacaton & Arine Aprahamian - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: Young Lebanese-Armenian architect Arine Aprahamian is inspired to create buildings that are innovative, affordable and sustainable. Her philosophy sits well with French Pritzker-Prize winning architect Anne Lacaton, who espouses building renewal over demolition.

WaPageTitle.value: Anne Lacaton & Arine Aprahamian - Rolex mentoring programme

metadescription.value: Young architect Arine Aprahamian is collaborating with mentor Anne Lacaton on a visionary project to revitalize Beirut's Bourj Hammoud quarter. More on rolex.org.

pageCaption.value: Young Filipino filmmaker Rafael Manuel collaborated for two years with acclaimed Chinese director Jia Zhang-Ke through the Rolex mentoring programme.

pageTitle.value: Bernardine Evaristo & Ayesha Harruna Attah - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: After finishing her studies in the US, author Ayesha Harruna Attah returned to Africa with a mission to write African stories for African readers. Five books later, she is ambitious for a wider readership.
